text:
A month or so ago I was thinking about the World Federation of Trade Unions (WFTU) – the new name for an international union grouping which has existed since 1945 but spent many years fairly below the labour horizon. They’re suddenly here, here in South Africa in any case. A bunch of trade unions affiliated to the Congress of South African Trade Unions (COSATU) have just this week affiliated to the WFTU and are campaigning for an immediate turn-round and for COSATU to affiliate as well. Others might see this as a possible step towards the long sought-after unity of the labour movement. I see instead a version of what used to be called “Stalinism”, but with all that implied by the term – leadership from on high, cult of personality and lack of any real grass roots – artificially nurtured in a hydroponic laboratory of dissent.

The World Federation of Trade Unions (WFTU) and its immediate predecessor, the Red International of Labour Unions (Profintern), have long-standing histories that underpin the contemporary make-up of the WFTU. Contemporary General Secretary of the WFTU, George Mavrikos, has sought to revise and re-present the WFTU as a progressive union body that promotes what he terms a ‘class-oriented’ revolutionary unionism that appeals to the interests of workers in developing contexts. However, it can be argued that the body of the organisation remains rooted in the legacy of Soviet unionism and, moreover, in the interests of a number of authoritarian regimes that have supported it such as China, Vietnam and Cuba. The more compromising, conciliatory and ‘less political’ agenda of the International Trade Union Confederation (ITUC) has created a space that the WFTU is attempting to occupy to the detriment of COSATU affiliated unions. The “fundamental difference” between the two, more than being a matter of divergence on union tactics, has to do with centuries old divergences: the one between unionism as a form of self-organisation of workers against capital and the other between unionism as a legacy of state control and authoritarianism. The chances for a successful and solidarity-driven workers’ international are minimal if one looks at the institutions that are contending for leadership of the labour movement. The strength of worker-to-worker solidarity lies much more in the level of workers’ activism, strikes and direct actions that express their immediate needs and that give meaning to any form of international solidarity at the level of the unions, rather than in the more formal structures that at present exist.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
